items.q: What happens to an enzyme when it is denatured?
items.correct: Its tertiary structure (3D shape) changes, particularly the active site, so it can no longer bind substrate effectively.
items.misconception: The enzyme has died because enzymes are alive.
items.random_wrong: The enzyme has been completely broken into amino acids.
items.why: Students think of enzymes as alive (probably because they are produced by living things), so they describe denaturation as death rather than shape change.

items.q: Why are enzymes specific to certain substrates?
items.correct: Only substrates with complementary shape and chemistry to the active site can bind effectively.
items.misconception: Each enzyme can react with any substrate, but works fastest with one type.
items.random_wrong: Enzymes choose their substrate based on which one is most common in the cell.
items.why: Students underestimate how strict the lock-and-key/induced-fit relationship is; they think specificity is a preference rather than a structural requirement.

items.q: What does a gene code for?
items.correct: Typically a polypeptide (protein) — through transcription to mRNA and translation by ribosomes.
items.misconception: A gene codes for a specific physical feature like eye colour directly.
items.random_wrong: Each gene codes for an organ or body part as a whole.
items.why: Students learn 'gene for eye colour' shorthand, then think genes directly produce traits rather than producing proteins that contribute to traits.

items.q: An enzyme stops working above its optimum temperature because:
items.why: Correct answer: its active site changes shape (denatures). “it dissolves” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.q: Which is the best optimum pH for pepsin (in stomach)?
items.why: It's pH 2. “pH 7” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: If an enzyme reaction takes 20 s at 25°C and 5 s at 35°C, how many times faster at 35°C?
items.why: Exact answer: 4 x. Estimation gets you near it; only the anchored number survives the exam hall.
